summaries.content: This lesson covers the poem 'Run!' which celebrates the joy of running and outdoor play. The poem uses short, lively lines and rhyming words to create an energetic mood. It encourages children to go outside, feel the breeze, run in the sun, and enjoy being active. The poem teaches that exercise and play are important for staying healthy and happy. Students learn about the poem's rhythm, rhyme scheme, and the cheerful message it carries.
keyPoints: The poem 'Run!' celebrates outdoor play and exercise
It uses rhyming words like run/sun and fun/done
The poem has a happy, energetic mood
Running and playing outdoors keeps us healthy and joyful

summaries.content: This lesson tells the humorous folk tale 'Nasruddin's Aim.' Mulla Nasruddin boasts to everyone that he is the greatest archer with perfect aim. When asked to prove his skills, he shoots three arrows. The first two miss the target badly - one goes far to the left and the other far to the right. Nasruddin cleverly explains that the first shot was how a general shoots and the second was how the king shoots. When the third arrow hits the target, he proudly says that is how Nasruddin shoots! The story teaches children about boasting and making excuses.
keyPoints: Nasruddin is a famous character from folk tales known for humorous stories
He boasted about being the best archer
His first two arrows missed the target completely
He made clever excuses by blaming the missed shots on others
The moral is to not boast and to accept mistakes honestly

summaries.content: This lesson teaches verbs - the action words in English grammar. A verb tells us what someone or something does. Every sentence must have at least one verb. Students learn to identify verbs in sentences from the poem and story, such as run, jump, shoot, miss, and hit. The lesson also introduces different forms of verbs: the base form (run), the form used with he/she/it (runs), and the -ing form (running). Children practise finding verbs in sentences and using them correctly.
keyPoints: A verb is an action word that tells what someone does
Every complete sentence needs at least one verb
Verbs change form: run, runs, running
Examples from the chapter: run, jump, shoot, miss, hit, aim, boast
